source_texts.title: Qheadache kickoff interview — gameplay, UI, and cross-platform considerations
source_texts.text:
Maya [Business Analyst]: Okay, thanks for joining. Let’s dive right in. This is the first long-form chat to nail down what we mean when we say “simple standalone sliding-block puzzle,” branded… well, we’re calling it Qheadache officially.

Victor [Product Owner]: Yeah, that name stuck. We’re not changing it. It’s Qheadache everywhere — the app title bar, About box, you name it.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Great, Qheadache it is. Desktop app, Qt. No server, right? I just want to be sure we aren’t sneaking in cloud saves or anything.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Correct, standalone. No back-end, no online bits. Single local user per machine. We’re keeping it clean.

Renee [QA Tester]: Single user as in… one profile? Like it doesn’t switch profiles inside the app? I’ll need that spelled out for testing.

Victor [Product Owner]: Exactly, one user context at a time. We’re not doing multiple logins or profiles. Just whoever is playing on that computer.

Sasha [Community Rep]: Which is fine for the audience. Kids and grown-ups will share the same PC sometimes, but they aren’t expecting profiles. They just want to play.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Speaking of the audience, we want it to be approachable for beginners, starting roughly 8 years old, up through puzzle-savvy adults. No special training, no thick manual. If you can use a mouse and keyboard, you’re good.

Sasha [Community Rep]: And they will use both. Some will have trackpads, some mice, even those weird pen devices. But basically move the cursor and click. Oh, and typing names when they win.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Input devices that can move a cursor and accept text entry should work fine in Qt: mouse, touchpad, trackball, pen, even some joystick-driven cursors technically. Keyboard for menu navigation and name entry is trivial.

Renee [QA Tester]: Good, I’ll test with mouse and touchpad at least. Now, the board. I need clarity on size. I’ve seen drafts saying “5x by 4x,” but is that width by height or the other way around?

Maya [Business Analyst]: Height is 5x, width is 4x. So it’s taller than it is wide. I know people get that reversed.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Got it. 5x tall, 4x wide. And the unit x is our layout scaling unit — constrained so that x stays between 50 and 100 pixels. We’ll pick an initial x based on the window size.

Victor [Product Owner]: We’re also saying it’s playable at 800x600 minimum. That’s non-negotiable. It needs to be clear at that resolution.

Renee [QA Tester]: I’m going to test on a Windows machine at 800x600 just to be sure, and then on a couple of Linux variants. But we have to run on Windows; folks keep asking for Windows builds in the community.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Running on all Qt-supported OSes is fine. Windows, Linux, macOS — but Windows portability is an explicit must-have.

Sasha [Community Rep]: Another thing people care about — visuals. We promised a black backdrop for the board, right? And the blocks are the bright yellow ones.

Victor [Product Owner]: Yes, black board, yellow blocks. Keep the blocks with a tiny spacing so they don’t stick to each other visually.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Spacing is 0.1x between blocks. It’s subtle but noticeable.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Okay, 0.1x gutters. I’ll enforce that consistently. Now, the pieces: remind me of the exact set.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Four small squares, each 1x by 1x.

Renee [QA Tester]: And four tall rectangles, 2x tall by x wide. Plus one wide rectangle, x tall by 2x wide, and the “big square,” which is 2x by 2x.

Sasha [Community Rep]: That’s the classic set folks expect. Don’t mess with the counts or the sizes — there was drama in another game when they changed it.

Victor [Product Owner]: We’re not changing it. It’s fixed.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Alright. Play interaction: we’re going with mouse-driven drag, but I want to confirm: when the player presses the left mouse button on a block, does that immediately put us into a moving state?

Maya [Business Analyst]: Yes. We’ve been calling it “block movement” state in notes. You’re dragging at that point.

Renee [QA Tester]: And when they let go of the left button over that block, we’re back to selection mode. That transition matters for testing stuck states.

Sasha [Community Rep]: People will expect the block to stick to the cursor while they’re holding it down. If it lags or jumps, they’ll complain.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: It’ll follow the mouse movements smoothly; I’ll make sure the drag is responsive.

Victor [Product Owner]: Also, we have to do menus. The main window has a menu bar — Stats, Help, the usual. Keep it straightforward, menu-driven. No hidden gestures or anything.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Right, graphics and menus. We can have some sound effects if you want, but the game must be fully playable muted. Actually, let’s make sure that if audio is off, nothing breaks.

Renee [QA Tester]: I’ll test with the OS muted too. I don’t want sound to be a dependency. We had that issue somewhere else where a missing audio driver froze a dialog. Not again.

Sasha [Community Rep]: And kids play on school computers with sound off; also some parents hate bleeps and bloops.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Menus: Stats should have two items — Display and Erase — in that order? I’ve seen both orders in screenshots from other apps.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Display, then Erase. When you choose Display, it pops up the scores window listing recorded players and their times and moves. If you choose Erase, it clears all stats.

Renee [QA Tester]: And the menu should close after the action is done. I don’t want sticky open menus confusing users.

Victor [Product Owner]: Yes, close the menu afterward. Same idea for Help -> About. Open About, then when you’re done, the Help menu shouldn’t stay open.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: About content — do we have the actual text? I need to embed it.

Maya [Business Analyst]: We do. The exact line is: “Qheadache 1.0 by Jean-Philippe Brossat jp_brossat@yahoo.fr”. That needs to be shown exactly like that in the About window.

Sasha [Community Rep]: It’s a nice touch to credit the original author. People in the community care about attribution.

Renee [QA Tester]: Okay, I’ll verify the exact characters, spaces, the email, everything. Now, stats: on victory we prompt for the player’s name, right? That’s when we record the time and action count together with the name.

Victor [Product Owner]: Yes. So you solve the puzzle, you get a name prompt. If it’s a kid, they might just type initials. But we need a field for it.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: We’ll have a modal dialog on victory with an entry field. Then we write out the score record: name, elapsed time, and action count.

Sasha [Community Rep]: People will want to see a list. So the scores window you mentioned needs to show all saved players sorted… actually, do we care about ordering?

Maya [Business Analyst]: Let’s not overcomplicate it right now. Just show the list. Sorting isn’t a requirement this round. The important part is we display all recorded scores.

Renee [QA Tester]: Undo/redo: we agreed to allow undoing and redoing a lot of moves. What’s the cap again?

Lars [Qt Programmer]: I can implement a ring buffer. But what’s the number we picked?

Maya [Business Analyst]: Up to the last 1000 actions. It’s high, but it stops memory growth.

Sasha [Community Rep]: 1000 is fine. People will brag they can step through their entire solution backward.

Victor [Product Owner]: That number’s a ceiling. We aren’t exposing that as a setting — at least not now.

Renee [QA Tester]: So action count is the number we present on screen during play too, right? I want to see it increment so kids can see how efficient they were.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Yes, I’ll surface both the elapsed time and the current move count somewhere in the main window UI — maybe a small status bar or a panel near the menu.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Make sure it’s readable at 800x600. Big clear digits. We’re designing for clarity, no clutter.

Sasha [Community Rep]: UI question: The board area itself is a black rectangle of 5x by 4x. If the window resizes, do we scale x between 50 and 100? What if the window gets huge?

Lars [Qt Programmer]: I’ll clamp x within 50–100 pixels. If the window is too big, we can keep the board centered with padding. If it’s too small to fit 50px per x, we block it — but the minimum app window will be at least 800x600 anyway, so it should be fine.

Renee [QA Tester]: I’ll test resizing scenarios. Also DPI scaling on Windows. But at base level, as long as our math on x is consistent, the blocks won’t collide visually thanks to that 0.1x gap.

Victor [Product Owner]: And the highlight or movement visuals: we’re not doing fancy shadows or filters. Keep it crisp. Yellow on black with clean spacing is the look.

Sasha [Community Rep]: Does the block actually “snap” to anything? People will complain if the drag sticks inside other blocks.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: It won’t overlap others. Movement is constrained so blocks slide freely where allowed; while moving, the selected block follows the cursor. When you release the left button, we switch back to selection. That’s the cycle.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Exactly. Press-down on a block puts you in moving mode; release returns you to normal. That matches what players expect.

Renee [QA Tester]: Let’s not forget menu operations are keyboard accessible too. Arrow keys and Enter should work to select Stats->Display, Stats->Erase, Help->About.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Standard Qt menu input covers that. Cursor input from keyboard and pointing devices is a must anyway.

Victor [Product Owner]: One more time on brand: the name Qheadache needs to appear in the title bar of the main window. Also in the About line we discussed.

Maya [Business Analyst]: And in the installer name and icon if we do one. But at minimum inside the app.

Sasha [Community Rep]: Scheduling stuff — sorry to derail — but I heard we’re aiming for a demo next month. I might be out the first week.

Renee [QA Tester]: That reminds me, I asked for budget to borrow a classroom laptop set. Some of them run old Windows and low resolutions. I’ll try 800x600 on one of those.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: The Qt build can target a wide range of OSes. Windows is a given, Linux is fine, macOS also. I’ll set up CI for at least Windows and Linux local builds. Not that we’re doing any server, but cross-OS testing is worthwhile.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Right. And remember, we’re writing our internal doc for our analysts, developers, and testers. QA will need crisp acceptance criteria especially around menu behavior and score recording.

Sasha [Community Rep]: Question: The game is play-without-sound friendly, but do we need a mute button? Or is it just silent by default?

Victor [Product Owner]: We can include simple sounds later, but for now keep in mind it must be fully playable with no audio at all. Whether there’s a toggle is secondary. The crucial thing is no dependency on sound.

Renee [QA Tester]: I’ll test with system-level mute and also if there’s an in-app mute. But yeah, must be playable silently.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Stats “Erase” — is that instant? Some apps confirm before wiping. I assume if we keep it simple, one activation clears everything and returns you with the menu closed.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Keep it simple. We don’t need a confirm dialog this phase; it’s a straightforward clear.

Sasha [Community Rep]: For the About, please make sure that exact text shows: Qheadache 1.0 by Jean-Philippe Brossat jp_brossat@yahoo.fr. People will take screenshots.

Renee [QA Tester]: I’ll literally compare the characters. Last project we had a missing dash and I nearly lost my mind.

Victor [Product Owner]: Game loop-wise, we’re solving a single “headache” layout. It’s not a level pack. Just the specific puzzle. That’s enough for the brand.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Right, we’re not doing multiple puzzles here. The UI should present the board and let users solve that specific configuration.

Sasha [Community Rep]: I’m okay with that. People new to sliding puzzles like a single goal. The advanced folks can chase lower move counts.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Speaking of move counts, I’m planning to show the elapsed time in mm:ss and the action count incrementing by 1 per move. Both visible at all times during play.

Renee [QA Tester]: And those values are exactly what we capture into a “score” when the player wins: the name, the time, and the move count.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Yes. Scores are then visible through the Stats menu when you choose Display. That opens a window listing all players with their recorded results.

Victor [Product Owner]: Menu closure: after you pick Display, the menu should disappear and leave you with the scores window. Don’t leave the menu stuck open.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Same pattern for Help->About. When you choose About, we pop the About window and the Help menu closes. Consistent behavior.

Sasha [Community Rep]: Do we need narration or voice? I assume no. Kids can read the menus.

Maya [Business Analyst]: No narration. Keep it minimal.

Renee [QA Tester]: Minor confusion: earlier I heard someone say 4x by 5x. Can we write that down one more time so it’s unambiguous in the test plan?

Maya [Business Analyst]: Height equals 5x, width equals 4x. Board rectangle sits in the main window, background black. Blocks are the yellow ones with 0.1x gaps between them.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Then the main window holds both the board and the menu bar. I’ll do a basic layout with the menu bar at the top, board centered, and a small status area for time and moves.

Victor [Product Owner]: Keep the interface menu-driven. People know File, Edit, etc., but here we mainly need the Statistics and Help menus. No context menus right now.

Sasha [Community Rep]: A community tangent: someone asked if we’re going to support multi-user sessions on the same machine. We already said single user, correct?

Maya [Business Analyst]: Single user per machine per session. That’s deliberate.

Renee [QA Tester]: Alright. Another tangent while we’re at it — we’re not doing network. I’m relieved. Testing servers is a headache, pun intended.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Desktop only. No server calls. All data local.

Victor [Product Owner]: Audio aside, do we need any special skills assumed for users? I’m thinking no. They need basic computer literacy only.

Sasha [Community Rep]: Exactly. If you can handle a mouse and type your name, you can play. Kids as young as eight should get it.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Let’s wrap with a quick recap: Qheadache is a desktop Qt game with a main window that has a menu bar and the puzzle board. The board is 5x tall, 4x wide, black background; blocks are yellow, spaced 0.1x apart. There are four 1x1 squares, four 2x by 1x rectangles, one 1x by 2x rectangle, and one 2x by 2x square. Play is mouse-driven: press left to enter movement, the block follows the cursor, release returns to selection. Keyboard and pointing devices work for cursor and menu and name entry. We track time and actions, show those during play, and on victory we prompt for a name and store that with the time and move count. Stats menu shows Display then Erase; Display opens the scores window listing all players; Erase clears stats; menus close after the action. Help->About opens an About window with the exact text — and the menu closes too. The app runs standalone across Qt-supported OSes including Windows. The UI is clear at 800x600. No audio dependency. Single local user. Audience is 8+ up to adult enthusiasts.

Renee [QA Tester]: Perfect summary. I’ll turn that into a test checklist.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: I’m good. I’ll start stubbing the main window, menus, board, and movement logic. We’ll wire up the score recording after I have the undo/redo buffer working — oh, undo/redo is 1000 actions maximum, I’ll implement the cap now.

Sasha [Community Rep]: I’m excited. Please make the drag feel smooth — people always complain about stutter.

Victor [Product Owner]: Smooth movement, reliable behavior, and a neat little About box. It’s going to be fine.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Great. If anything is unclear, ping me. But I think we’re aligned: no special training required, accessible, and menu-driven. Thanks, everyone.

Renee [QA Tester]: Thanks. I’ll go schedule the Windows tests.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: I’ll push the first Qt project template this afternoon.

Sasha [Community Rep]: I’ll start drafting the community announcement once we have screenshots.

Victor [Product Owner]: Let’s reconvene next week for a quick demo of the main window and the drag behavior.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Done. Meeting adjourned. Actually, wait—one last thing: make sure the app window title says Qheadache. Not QHeadache or something else.

Lars [Qt Programmer]: Noted: exact capitalization, Qheadache. I’ll set it now before I forget.

Renee [QA Tester]: I’ll check that too. See you all.

Sasha [Community Rep]: Bye!

Victor [Product Owner]: Later.

Maya [Business Analyst]: Thanks, all.

source_texts.title: Raw notes — UI layout, menus, gameplay states, and stats (Design sync 2025-03-18)
source_texts.text:
Date: 2025-03-18
Call link: https://meet.example.com/qheadache-design-sync
Attendees:
- Maya (BA)
- Lars (Qt dev)
- Renee (QA)
- Victor (PO)
- Sasha (Community)

Notes (messy):
- Name: keep official product name as Qheadache. window title, About text, even Save file name if any (but we are NOT doing saves now) [TBD] —> but name Qheadache everywhere.
- Domain: sliding-block desktop puzzle. one “headache” puzzle only. UI should help you solve that particular layout (not multiple levels). People will try to solve faster/fewer moves.
- App type: standalone exe/app bundle. No server calls. local-only data. Single user per machine session (no profile switching in-app). [Renee asked: do we ever need user management?] Answer: No. Single local user only.
- UI approach: menus + graphics + optional audio. Must be fully playable with sound off or system muted. Keep it simple. No audio dependency.
- Main window: menu bar + board area + status region (time, actions). Keep menu-driven interactions. (No fancy context menu for now; keep top bar.)
- Board geometry: rectangle height 5x, width 4x. Black background. Fit inside window with some padding. x is layout unit. clamp x within 50–100 pixels. If window big, pad; if small, enforce min window to avoid x < 50.
- Colors: blocks yellow. Board black. (briefly debated inverted colors — crossed out) NOT decided yet (actually decided: keep black board, yellow blocks). [Update: stick to original plan]
- Spacing: blocks separated by ~0.1x “gutters”. So visually not touching. Keep consistent around all blocks. (Asked: margin to border as well? Possibly same 0.1x to outside? [TBD], but requirement is block-to-block spacing at least.)
- Preset set (fixed):
  * 4 small squares (1x by 1x)
  * 4 tall rectangles (2x high, x wide)
  * 1 wide rectangle (x high, 2x wide)
  * 1 big square (2x by 2x)
  counts are fixed. no variants. [Someone suggested more pieces — crossed out]
- Interaction model (pointer-based):
  * Left mouse down on a block => enter movement state (“block movement”).
  * While in movement, block should track cursor position. Constraint rules prevent overlap.
  * On left button release (on that selected block): return to selection state. (Note: confirm nothing odd on release off the board; still return to selection.)
  * People may use other pointing devices (trackpads etc.). Should still move the block with pointer; keyboard support for menus and entering name.
- Input devices: keyboard, mouse, touchpad, trackball, etc., anything that moves an on-screen cursor. Use keyboard for menu nav and for victory name entry. (No touch/gesture specifics in scope.)
- Resolution: 800x600 minimum. At that size, digits for time and action counts must be legible. Use Qt font scaling as needed. Renee to test on 800x600 Windows.
- Frameworks: Qt required. Cross-platform — runs on OS supported by Qt. Windows support is explicitly required. Also test on Linux; mac if possible.
- Undo/Redo: provide history up to last 1000 actions. Discussion: is 1000 enough? Yes. Not user-configurable now. Use ring buffer (Lars: implement). [Renee: ensure redo resets correctly when new moves are made.] 
- Progress tracking: show elapsed time during a game session. Show action count incrementing per move. Present near the board in a non-intrusive area. Keep digits large.
- On win (victory condition reached): prompt for player name. Record: player name + elapsed time + action count. Save locally. (No online sync.)
- Statistics: accessible via menu. “Statistics” menu contains two items. Order matters: Display first, Erase second. Accept both mouse and keyboard selection. On Display => open scores window with list of recorded players (all entries visible). On Erase => wipe stored stats (no confirmation for now; keep it simple). After performing either action, the Statistics menu should close — not remain open.
- Help menu: contains About item. Accept keyboard and mouse selection. On About => open About window. The Help menu closes after About is shown. About text must exactly read: Qheadache 1.0 by Jean-Philippe Brossat jp_brossat@yahoo.fr (Verify exact spaces/characters). [Renee to test literal text]
- Single-user: Only one user per machine context. Stats are shared on the machine (no per-user profiles). Note: QA to confirm behavior if OS user accounts differ; scope is single user in-app per session. [But we are not preventing multiple OS users; just no multi-profile in app.]
- Audience assumptions: No special qualifications required. Basic computer users only. Kids as young as 8 should manage without training. Adults (puzzle enthusiasts) also targeted.
- Menu behavior consistency: After executing an action (Stats->Display or Erase; Help->About), the menu should close. Avoid persistent dropdowns post-action. (Renee: add test case for that.)

Side convos / contradictions / clarifications:
- “Is board 4x wide by 5x tall or 5x wide by 4x tall?” — Answer finalized: height 5x, width 4x. (Earlier note had it reversed — crossed out.) Document clearly.
- “Spacing 0.1x: Is that exactly 0.1x or at least 0.1x?” — We say maintain 0.1x between blocks (exact). Border padding [TBD].
- “Sound effects?” — Keep optional. Must be playable with sound off. No requirement for sound now.
- “Sorting scores by time?” — Not in scope. Just display all recorded players. [Future? Parking Lot]
- “DPI scaling on Windows?” — Test at 100%, 125% if possible; the 800x600 requirement is physical resolution; UI must remain usable. [QA note]
- “Input alternatives?” — Accept keyboard and pointing devices that can move on-screen cursor + name entry. No voice control. No gamepad-specific mapping [out of scope].
- “Menus closing rule conflicts with About staying open?” — Clarification: The menu itself closes, About window remains until dismissed, which is fine. Not contradictory.
- “Undo/Redo 1000 actions vs memory usage?” — 1000 is cap. Should be fine. Implementation uses compact move representation. [Lars: Monitor memory in debug.]
- “Single-user vs OS profiles?” — In-app single-user stance stands. Stats are local to machine’s storage (or current OS user profile storage) — but app doesn’t differentiate users inside UI. [Renee: test new Windows profile -> separate stats file probably, but still no in-app selection.]

Action items:
- [Lars] Implement main window with menu bar, black board area (5x by 4x), status line w/ time & action count (legible at 800x600). Use Qt.
- [Lars] Pieces: four 1x1 squares, four 2x by 1x vertical rectangles, one 1x by 2x horizontal rectangle, one 2x by 2x square — all yellow; maintain 0.1x spacing. Ensure movement constraints prevent overlap.
- [Lars] Input: left mouse down enters movement; while moving, block follows cursor; on left mouse up over selection, return to selection mode. Keyboard and pointer support for menus; keyboard entry for victory name.
- [Lars] Undo/Redo history support up to 1000 actions. Provide UI hooks for QA to test.
- [Lars] Statistics menu items: Display (shows scores window), Erase (clears all). Menu closes after action. Help menu: About item; menu closes after showing About. About text must be exact string (see above).
- [Renee] Prepare test plan for Windows (portable), Linux; test at 800x600; system audio muted tests; DPI scaling tests; verify menu close behavior, About exact text, Stats erase function.
- [Maya] Write short spec aimed at analysts, developers, and QA testers. Clarify board dimensions, piece set, input model, menu structure, data captured on victory.
- [Sasha] Draft community blurb explaining that Qheadache is a simple desktop puzzle, easy for kids and adults, no sign-in, shows time and moves, and keeps local scores.

Open Issues / Parking Lot:
- Sorting or filtering scores — NOT in current release. [Park]
- Confirm whether border-to-block margin equals 0.1x or something else [TBD]. For now, only block-to-block spacing is committed.
- Visual feedback on selection (glow/outline?) — Keep minimal. If any highlight is added, must not contradict yellow-on-black clarity. [TBD]
- Confirmation on Erase? Current stance: no confirm. Might add in future if user feedback says accidental erasure occurs. [Park]
- Localization — Not in scope. About text is fixed English line. Menus English for now. [Park]
- Platform packaging for macOS — If time permits. Windows build is priority (explicit portability). [TBD]

Random bits from call (not fully captured):
- Someone asked if we could add a hint system — crossed out. Out of scope. Keep puzzle pure.
- There was confusion over “x unit range 50-100 px.” Clarify: x is clamped; actual x used depends on window size. At 800x600 we will pick an x that fits board + UI. Might be around ~80 px, but exact depends on margins.
- Reminder: End users assumed to know basic PC ops (move cursor, click, type). No special qualifications beyond that. Audience includes 8+ kids and adult puzzle enthusiasts.
- Recording score on victory: ensure we capture name, elapsed time, action count. The score window lists players. Do not require login. No network. Standalone.
- The “headache” puzzle is the specific layout we provide. No level select. The UI is there to enable solving that one arrangement by sliding blocks.
- Menu acceptance input: both mouse clicks and keyboard navigation must trigger items. (Renee to check keyboard focus order.)

Potential contradictions flagged by Renee:
- One note said “board 5x by 4x, black bg,” another side note scribbled “4x by 5x landscape?” — We resolved it: height 5x, width 4x (portrait). Cross out landscape variant.
- Erase with confirm vs erase without confirm — For now, without confirm. Add to Open Issues in case we revisit.
- Spacing constant vs responsive — Keep constant: 0.1x. Not relative to window size beyond x.

Misc:
- Mind the title: Qheadache. Not “QHeadache,” watch casing in UI.
- Help->About shows exact text: Qheadache 1.0 by Jean-Philippe Brossat jp_brossat@yahoo.fr. (Exact. No trailing period.)
- The Stats window is a separate window/dialog. It displays all scores — players’ names, the times, and action counts. We don’t need to specify column order now. [Future: sort?]
- Undo/redo up to 1000 actions — ensure redo is cleared when a new move is made after undo. QA to validate.
- The app is menu-driven; keep consistency: menus close after action selection (Stats, Help). No hidden shortcuts required for basic play.

Wrap-up:
- Deliver a Qt desktop app (Qheadache) that runs on Windows and other Qt-supported OSes. Minimum 800x600, black 5x by 4x board with yellow blocks spaced 0.1x. Specific piece set fixed. Mouse-driven dragging: press to move, release to stop; block follows cursor. Show time and action counts. On victory, prompt for name; record name + time + actions. A Statistics menu with Display, Erase allows showing the scores window and clearing stats; menu closes afterward. Help->About displays the exact credit line; Help menu closes after. Single local user. No special training required. Audience: beginners (8+) and adults. Standalone, no back-end.
- Next steps: Lars to prototype; Renee to prep tests; Maya to refine spec (analysts/dev/testers). Sasha on messaging later.

source_texts.title: Subject drift: Qheadache menus, About text, Windows portability, and undo cap
source_texts.text:
Email 1
From: Victor <victor@studio.example>
To: Maya <maya@studio.example>, Lars <lars@studio.example>
CC: Renee <renee@studio.example>, Sasha <sasha@community.example>
Date: 2025-04-02 09:12
Subject: Qheadache basics + menu behavior

Hey team,

Quick check before I hit send on a brief for stakeholders. Three things I want to lock:

1) The app is a desktop game (no server), official name “Qheadache.” Please keep the brand consistent in the app title and About. We’re not doing any multiplayer or profile juggling. It’s just one user per machine/session.

2) Minimum screen: 800x600. We promised that. Also, menu-driven UI. People click “Statistics” for scores and “Help” for About. Stats should have two items, Display and Erase, in that order. When they click either, don’t leave the menu hanging open.

3) About box: I need the exact text used. I’ll paste that in the mini-brief if you send it over again, Maya.

Also remind me, the move counter and timer are always visible, right? I recall we wanted to show progress as they play.

-Victor

---

Email 2
From: Maya <maya@studio.example>
To: Victor <victor@studio.example>
CC: Lars <lars@studio.example>, Renee <renee@studio.example>, Sasha <sasha@community.example>
Date: 2025-04-02 09:33
Subject: RE: Qheadache basics + menu behavior

Hi Victor,

Confirming:
- Desktop, standalone (no back-end). One local user only.
- Yes, min 800x600.
- UI is menus + graphics; sound optional and not required to play.
- Stats menu items order is Display then Erase. On Display we show the scores window listing all recorded players; Erase clears the stored stats. After either, the Statistics menu should close automatically.
- Help -> About: open About window; Help menu closes. The line to display (exact) is: Qheadache 1.0 by Jean-Philippe Brossat jp_brossat@yahoo.fr
- Timer + action count both visible during play. We count actions as moves.

Also, for the main window: a black rectangular board (5x tall, 4x wide) with yellow blocks separated by 0.1x spacing. x constrained between 50–100 px so we don’t get micro-blocks or giant blocks. That board plus the menu bar should cover the basic UI.

We’re writing the spec aimed at analysts, programmers, and testers (internal), so I’ll phrase it accordingly in the doc.

Maya

---

Email 3
From: Lars <lars@studio.example>

Subject: RE: Qheadache basics + menu behavior (Qt details + undo cap)

All,

On the technical side with Qt:
- I’ll ensure portability across Qt-supported OS — but yes, Windows is explicitly supported. I’ll test a Windows build early.
- The “x” unit will be clamped 50 <= x <= 100 px. At 800x600, the board (5x by 4x) will fit with status text.
- Mouse interaction: left-button press on a block enters move mode; while held, the block tracks the cursor; on release we revert to selection. Keyboard input remains for menus; and obviously text input for name entry on victory.
- Undo/redo history: I’m setting the cap to 1000 moves. Any pushback? It’s large enough but bounded. I’ll implement a circular buffer and ensure redo clears when a new move is made after undo.

Two clarifications I need before I finalize the About dialog:
1) We’re keeping the menu closing behavior consistent after About is opened, correct? I’ll set it so the Help menu closes immediately when About opens.
2) The About text line is exactly: Qheadache 1.0 by Jean-Philippe Brossat jp_brossat@yahoo.fr (no punctuation at the end)? I’ll hard-code that exact string.

-Lars

Adding a bit more (sorry for the thread drift):

- “Statistics -> Erase” should clear all scores. Let’s keep it simple; no confirmation dialog in this release. And yes, the menu should close after either Display or Erase performs its action.
- Help -> About: uses the exact credit line that’s been circulating. Confirmed again.
- Undo and redo up to 1000 actions is still the plan. If anyone disagrees, shout now.

Drag smoothness is good on my Linux box; I’m pushing a Windows build tonight to confirm. With Qt we should be portable across supported platforms, and Windows is specifically in scope.

On layout specifics:
- I enshrined the 5x (height) by 4x (width) board rectangle, with x constrained to [50, 100] px. If the window is resized, I clamp x accordingly and center the board.
- The block spacing is a fixed 0.1x gap visually. The board is black, blocks are yellow, matching what we promised.
- For the main window, menus are in a top bar; time and action count appear on the right in a small status strip. At 800x600, the digits are readable, but I’ll wait for Renee to test for real.
- Input: Left mouse press enters move; the selected block follows the pointer; left release switches us back to the non-move mode. Keyboard will work for menu selections; we also accept typing the player’s name on the win dialog.

I’ll ping once the Windows build is ready for testing.

-Lars

candidate_pool.text: The system shall display a score window listing all player scores.
The system shall close the Help menu after displaying the About window.
When the user selects "About" from the Help menu, the system shall display an About window containing the text "Qheadache 1.0 by Jean-Philippe Brossat jp_brossat@yahoo.fr".
The system shall be playable without sound.
When the user selects "Erase" in the Statistics menu, the system shall erase the stored statistics.
The system shall be named "Qheadache".
The system shall run as a standalone application.
Users shall not be required to have any special qualifications to use the product.
Users are assumed to know how to operate a computer.
The system shall count and display the number of actions performed by a user.
The system shall accept input from a keyboard, a mouse, and other pointing or input devices that allow moving an on-screen cursor and entering names.

The system shall provide a Game menu containing a "Reset" item that, when selected, restores the board to the initial puzzle configuration, sets the elapsed time display to 00:00, sets the action count display to 0, and clears the undo/redo history.
The system shall provide a Pause command that suspends the elapsed time counter and disables block selection and movement until the user activates Resume.
The system shall display a user interface that enables solving a specific headache.
The system shall toggle between windowed and borderless fullscreen display modes when the user presses F11.
The system shall allow the user to save a PNG image of the current board and status area to a file path chosen via a file-save dialog.
The system shall include one block with height x and width 2x.
The system shall support zoom controls that adjust the layout unit x in 5-pixel steps within the 50–100 pixel bounds when the user presses Ctrl+'+' to zoom in and Ctrl+'-' to zoom out.
The system shall export all recorded scores to a CSV file with a header row "Name,Time,Actions" to a location chosen by the user.
The system shall record a play's score including its play time and action count associated with the player's name.
The system shall restrict the measurement unit x to be between 50 and 100 pixels.
The system shall be implemented as a computerized game.
The system shall require a graphical display supporting at least 800x600 resolution.
The system shall provide a Help menu containing an "About" item.
The system shall provide a Reset command that returns all blocks to their initial layout and clears the elapsed time and action count for the current game.
The system shall measure and display the elapsed time that a user spends playing a game.
The system shall allow a user to undo and redo up to the last 1000 actions.
The system shall render the board background in black and render the blocks in yellow.
The system shall include one square block with side length 2x.
The system shall use the Qt graphical library for its graphical interface and shall run on all operating systems supported by Qt.
The system shall accept menu selection input for the Help menu.
The system shall render the game board as a rectangle with height equal to 5x and width equal to 4x.
Users are assumed to include beginning-level players starting at age 8 and advanced players including adults.
The system shall accept menu selection input for the Statistics menu.
When the user selects "Display" in the Statistics menu, the system shall display the statistics window.
The system shall provide an optional grid overlay on the board that can be toggled on and off, rendering thin grid lines aligned to x-sized cells.
While in the "Block movement" state, the system shall move the selected block in response to mouse movement.
When the user releases the left mouse button on a selected block, the system shall change the game state to "Block selection".
The system shall support only one user per machine.
The system shall provide a user interface that uses menus, graphics, and sounds.
When the user presses the left mouse button down on a block, the system shall change the game state to "Block movement".
The system shall close the Statistics menu after performing the selected action.
The system shall separate blocks on the board with a margin of 0.1x.
The system shall include four square blocks each with side length x.
The system shall include four rectangular blocks each with height 2x and width x.
The software shall be portable to the Windows operating system.
The system shall provide a Statistics menu containing the items "Display" and "Erase" in that order.
The system shall provide a main window that contains a board and a menu bar.
The specification document shall be intended for analysts, programmers, and testers.

seed_registry.requirement_text: The system shall provide a Pause command that suspends the elapsed time counter and disables block selection and movement until the user activates Resume.
seed_registry.unsupported_reason: No pause or resume functionality is mentioned or implied in the sources.

seed_registry.requirement_text: The system shall toggle between windowed and borderless fullscreen display modes when the user presses F11.
seed_registry.unsupported_reason: Fullscreen mode and related shortcuts are not discussed anywhere in the sources.

seed_registry.requirement_text: The system shall allow the user to save a PNG image of the current board and status area to a file path chosen via a file-save dialog.
seed_registry.unsupported_reason: There is no mention of screenshot or image export capabilities in the sources.

seed_registry.requirement_text: The system shall export all recorded scores to a CSV file with a header row "Name,Time,Actions" to a location chosen by the user.
seed_registry.unsupported_reason: Score listing and erasure are specified, but exporting scores to files is never mentioned.

seed_registry.requirement_text: The system shall provide a Reset command that returns all blocks to their initial layout and clears the elapsed time and action count for the current game.
seed_registry.unsupported_reason: A reset or restart feature is not described in the sources.

seed_registry.requirement_text: The system shall support zoom controls that adjust the layout unit x in 5-pixel steps within the 50–100 pixel bounds when the user presses Ctrl+'+' to zoom in and Ctrl+'-' to zoom out.
seed_registry.unsupported_reason: User-controlled zoom adjustments are not specified; only automatic clamping of x is described.

seed_registry.requirement_text: The system shall provide an optional grid overlay on the board that can be toggled on and off, rendering thin grid lines aligned to x-sized cells.
seed_registry.unsupported_reason: No grid overlay or similar visual aid is mentioned in the sources.

seed_registry.requirement_text: The system shall provide a Game menu containing a "Reset" item that, when selected, restores the board to the initial puzzle configuration, sets the elapsed time display to 00:00, sets the action count display to 0, and clears the undo/redo history.
seed_registry.unsupported_reason: The source texts specify only the Statistics and Help menus and describe their items; no Reset functionality or Game menu is mentioned, and no mechanism is defined to restore the initial configuration or clear timers, counts, or history.

users: Casual PC user with basic computer skills
business_goals: Deliver a simple standalone sliding-block puzzle branded as Qheadache
Be approachable for kids and adults without special training
Run reliably on Windows and other platforms supported by Qt
Track and show progress via time and action counts
Keep interaction menu-driven with graphics and optional audio
core_features: Main window with a menu bar and a black rectangular board sized 5x by 4x; yellow blocks kept 0.1x apart
Preset piece set: four 1x1 squares, four 2x1 rectangles, one 1x2 rectangle, and one 2x2 square
Mouse-driven play: press on a block enters a movement state; releasing returns to selection; block follows cursor while moving
Input via keyboard and pointing devices for cursor control and name entry
Undo and redo history supporting up to the last 1000 actions
On victory, prompt for player name and record name, elapsed time, and action count as a score; a scores window lists recorded players

Menus: Statistics menu with Display then Erase (Display shows scores window, Erase clears stats; menu closes after action); Help menu with About showing the exact version/author line and closing afterward
quality_concerns: Portable across Qt-supported operating systems, explicitly including Windows
Usable at 800x600 or higher, clear for children and adults
Responsive drag interactions with smooth block movement
Fully playable with sound muted (no audio dependency)
constraints: Qt graphical framework required; desktop app runs wherever Qt is supported
Layout unit x constrained between 50 and 100 pixels
terminology: x: layout scaling unit for board and pieces
Blocks: movable pieces in specified sizes
Block movement / Block selection: interaction states
Action: a user move counted toward history
